title: Placing Archival Maps
section: A virtual exhibit using the ArcGIS Storymap platform.
text:
A virtual exhibit using the ArcGIS Storymap platform.
With this approach, you would include map(s) from your main object along with 6-8 other items from special collections. These items can provide context that help answer the questions above, explaining how these maps fit within the project and a broader corpus of materials or the historical context that helps explain either how they were created or how they and their larger project shaped (or didn’t shape urban space). They can also provide more information on the groups/organizations behind the creation of these maps or the process by which they were made. Or, lastly, you can consider alternative voices–those who may have seen these maps unfavorably whose voices are captured in archival materials or those are absent (which is particularly challenging).
This storymap would be structured in the following way:

A couple of paragraphs providing an introduction to your exhibit. This can include a description of the map(s) that are the main focus, the central theme you want to emphasize about those maps, and anything else that a viewer should keep in mind in looking through the artifacts you’ve collected.
A photo/image, citation, and caption for your main map(s) and each artifact in your exhibit. The photo can be from your phone, or you can use a digitized version if available. The citation should be in APA format–here’s one guide to using that for archival materials. We will discuss captions in class, but they should be relatively short (no more than a paragraph) and provide notable details/features that viewers should pay attention to and an explanation of how this artifact fits within the broader exhibit. Here’s one chapter on writing good captions.
Lastly, you should have a final section of a few paragraphs that situates the exhibit you’ve created within the context of our class. What are the connections to our readings thus far in the course? How does this exhibit help sharpen our thinking about the role of maps in urban spaces and urban life? Think of it as an explanation of how your exhibit fits within the museum (our class).

title: Placing Archival Maps
section: A visual mosaic of map(s) from your main item along with other relevant archival materials.
text:
A visual mosaic of map(s) from your main item along with other relevant archival materials.
With this approach, think of the conversation we had with Meghan Kelly and the work of Margaret Pearce on maps like “They Would Not Take Me There”. Or you could think of work to reinterpret historical monuments such as confederate statueshere in the Southeast. The goal for those cartographers is using visuals and text to situate maps, to understand the positionalities of mappers and those being mapped. The goal is to show how the map itself is not an objective mirror of reality but a historically and culturally specific visual object. It’s to call attention to aspects of these maps we might otherwise take for granted.
You can either digitally or physically alter your map while also adding the same type of archival materials or other text/visuals described above to help contextualize it. Here, too, you should think about a story you want to tell about your map–how it shows the world in ways that reflect its intended purpose, time, and place. Your group can consider what this might look like. I’ve got funds to print things out if you want.
If you choose option 2, I’d also ask your group to provide an interpretative guide of no more than 2 single spaced pages that provides an introduction to your visual (what’s its title?, what’s the main focus?) as well as walking us through your design. It should also cite any items your group used (see information on how to do that in choice 1 above)

title: Maps and the City
text:
About the course
Urban spaces are incessantly mapped. While maps of cities have existed for thousands of years, maps as a tool to understand and control urban life are a relatively recent development. In this class, we’ll be studying this process. Starting in the late 1800s, we’ll look at how social scientists, urban planners, and policymakers have mapped the urban social landscape. We’ll identify how the ways that we represent urban space have changed since that time. Lastly, we’ll also consider ways that counter-mapping and other alternative cartographies have been used to advocate for issues related to social justice and equity.
This class is primarily rooted in critical cartography, which focuses on how maps operate in society from a functional and symbolic perspective rather than only on formal elements of map design. We will also spend almost four weeks this semester in UGA’s Special Collections library, so you’ll have a chance to develop skills related to archival and primary research.


Learning outcomes
By the end of the course, students should be able to:

Explain the historical role of maps and spatial analysis in shaping urban policies and development, primarily in the United States. (Units 1 & 2)
Better understand the methods used for mapping and analyzing urban communities. (All units)
Gain critical literacy as a map reader and user through using techniques from critical cartography. (Units 1, 2, and 3)
Describe how maps can be used to facilitate urban governance as well as for community activism. (Units 2 and 4)
Develop their own positions on ethical issues related to mapping and spatial technologies in urban settings. (All units)
Improve their ability to understand, synthesize, and respond to academic research on urban geography. (Units 2 and 4)
